questions.question_text: Under Florida's homestead protection, a residence inside a municipality is protected on up to how much land?
questions.explanation: Inside a municipality, Florida homestead protection covers up to 1/2 acre regardless of the home's value. Outside a municipality, the protection extends to 160 acres.

questions.question_text: A judgment creditor wants to force the sale of a debtor's homestead property to satisfy a $200,000 civil judgment. Can the creditor do this?
questions.correct_answer: No, homestead is protected from forced sale by creditors
distractors: Yes, if the homestead value exceeds the debt
Yes, with a court order from a circuit judge
questions.explanation: Florida's homestead is protected from forced sale by judgment creditors regardless of property value. Exceptions exist only for property taxes, mortgage liens, mechanics' liens, and HOA/condo liens.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is an exception to Florida's homestead protection from forced sale?
questions.explanation: Homestead protection has four exceptions: property taxes, mortgage liens, mechanics' liens, and HOA/condo assessment liens. General creditors (credit cards, medical bills, student loans) cannot force a sale.

questions.question_text: A homestead property outside a municipality in Florida is protected from creditors on up to how many acres?
questions.explanation: Outside a municipality, Florida homestead protection extends to 160 acres, regardless of property value. This generous acreage limit is one of the most protective homestead provisions in the nation.

questions.question_text: Florida's homestead protection from forced sale is established by which legal authority?
questions.correct_answer: Article X, Section 4 of the FL Constitution
distractors: Chapter 475, Part I of Florida Statutes
Chapter 196, Part II of Florida Statutes
FREC Rule 61J2 of the Administrative Code
questions.explanation: Homestead protection from forced sale is a constitutional right under Article X, Section 4 of the Florida Constitution. This makes it one of the strongest homestead protections in the country.

questions.question_text: What is the total Florida homestead tax exemption on assessed property value?
questions.explanation: The total homestead tax exemption is $50,000. The first $25,000 applies to all taxing authorities. The second $25,000 (on value between $50,000-$75,000) applies to all taxing authorities except the school district.

questions.question_text: By what date must a Florida homeowner file for the homestead tax exemption?
questions.explanation: The homestead exemption application must be filed with the county property appraiser by March 1 of the tax year. The applicant must be a permanent Florida resident as of January 1.

questions.question_text: Under the Save Our Homes cap, annual increases in assessed value of homesteaded property are limited to:
questions.explanation: The Save Our Homes (SOH) cap limits annual assessed value increases to 3% or the Consumer Price Index (CPI), whichever is less. This protects homeowners from rapid tax increases in rising markets.

questions.question_text: The second $25,000 of Florida's homestead tax exemption does NOT apply to which taxing authority?
questions.explanation: The first $25,000 exemption applies to all taxing authorities. The second $25,000 (on assessed value between $50,000 and $75,000) applies to all authorities except the school district.

questions.question_text: A property's assessed value increases by 8% in one year. Under Save Our Homes, the homesteaded owner's taxable value increase is capped at:
questions.correct_answer: 3% or the CPI, whichever is less
distractors: The full increase with no cap
questions.explanation: Even if market values rise sharply, the SOH cap limits the assessed value increase to 3% or CPI (whichever is less). The difference between the market value and capped value is the SOH benefit.

questions.question_text: A married man wants to sell his homestead property that is titled solely in his name. What is required?
questions.correct_answer: His spouse must also sign the deed
distractors: A court order authorizing the sale
questions.explanation: Under Florida law, a married homestead owner cannot sell, mortgage, or transfer the homestead without the spouse's signature, even if the property is titled solely in one spouse's name. This is a commonly tested rule.

questions.question_text: A homeowner dies and tries to devise the homestead to a friend, bypassing the surviving spouse. Under Florida law, this devise is:
questions.correct_answer: Invalid — the surviving spouse has protected rights
distractors: Valid if stated in a notarized will
Valid if the friend is a family member
questions.explanation: A Florida homestead cannot be devised (willed) away from a surviving spouse or minor children. The surviving spouse has the right to a life estate or an undivided one-half interest as tenant in common.

questions.question_text: A Florida homeowner can transfer up to how much in accumulated Save Our Homes savings to a new homestead?
questions.explanation: Florida allows portability of SOH savings up to $500,000 to a new homestead anywhere in Florida. The new homestead must be established within 3 years of abandoning the old one.

questions.question_text: To transfer Save Our Homes portability benefits, a homeowner must establish a new homestead within what time frame?
questions.correct_answer: 3 years of abandoning the old homestead
distractors: 1 year of abandoning the old homestead
2 years of abandoning the old homestead
5 years of abandoning the old homestead
questions.explanation: The new homestead must be established within 3 years of abandoning the previous one to transfer up to $500,000 in accumulated SOH savings. After 3 years, the portability benefit is lost.

questions.question_text: A married homeowner wants to take out a home equity loan on the homestead. Which statement is correct?
questions.correct_answer: Both spouses must sign the mortgage
distractors: Only the titled spouse needs to sign
questions.explanation: Just as with selling, a married homeowner cannot mortgage the homestead without the spouse's signature. This protection exists even if only one spouse holds title to the property.
